channel.title: Continuum Archive Index — Global
channel.description: Canonical chronological index of published works (newest 500).

item.title: Why Trading Still Matters — And What Most People Get Wrong
item.description: Short-term trading is often dismissed as a zero-sum game, driven by hype and luck. But for those who approach it with discipline and conviction, trading can be a meaningful craft — a way to fund deeper work, align with values, and sharpen focus. In this piece, I share why I only trade equities (not crypto or forex), how I avoid signal-following hype, and why consistent, small gains often matter more than chasing spikes. This isn’t gambling — it’s Proof of Work.

item.title: Beyond the Timeline - A Vision for YOUR Continuum
How Do We Build Digital Lives That We Actually Own?
item.description: Bitcoin taught us to think differently about money. This article explores what happens when we apply that same principle to the rest of our digital lives. From Nostr and local-first software to relationship memory and AI, I argue that our writing, conversations, and digital history should begin with us—not with a platform.

item.title: Relational Memory
item.description: Continuum's media library has taken a step beyond simple file management. By tracking where each image is used across articles, notes, and profile metadata, it can protect published content, prevent accidental deletions, and surface relationships that would otherwise be hidden. This is the beginning of what I call relational memory—software that remembers not just what you've created, but how everything you've created fits together.

item.title: Digital Independence Day
item.description: What does independence mean in the digital age? Reflecting on America's 250th birthday and recent changes to Continuum, this article explores why true software sovereignty comes from owning the architecture rather than becoming dependent on any single provider or platform.

item.title: Continuum Media: Owning Your Images without Owning the Infrastructure
Sovereignty Isn't Really About Self-Hosting
item.description: Software sovereignty isn't just about self-hosting—it's about preserving the freedom to change infrastructure without rewriting your application. Using Continuum's migration from GitHub Pages to Cloudflare Pages as a real-world example, this article explores why interchangeable services matter more than any single provider and argues that true ownership comes from controlling your namespace and your ability to move your data.

item.title: Continuum Vault: Rethinking Digital Identity
item.description: This document outlines the current architectural direction for Continuum Vault. It communicates the long-term vision and guiding principles rather than every implementation detail. As Continuum evolves, this document will evolve alongside it.

item.title: Building Continuum — The Wardrobe
item.description: For years, my digital life was scattered across Facebook, LinkedIn, X, WhatsApp, Nostr, and countless other services. Each knew a different part of me, but none ever felt like home. This is the story of the question that ultimately became Continuum.

item.title: Where is Your Center of Gravity ?
Queue-Driven Archive Persistence in Continuum
item.description: Continuum’s archive system has now shifted from a simple “export current DB state” model to a queue-driven archival pipeline that preserves full event history over time. Events are queued as immutable raw snapshots, committed into Git-backed archive repositories, and later used to rebuild a global archive index independently from the live SQLite database. This architecture now supports durable version preservation, retry-safe archival processing, and long-term reconstruction of archive state entirely from committed history.

item.title: Syncing Events to Archive
item.description: Continuum’s archive sync work is moving from shell-driven Docker workflows into the application runtime itself. Step 1 proved that kind:1 notes can be persisted into the archive. Step 2 extends the same flow to kind:30023 long-form articles.

item.title: My First Real Day (On Stacker News)
AI As Assistant vs AI Slop
item.description: There is a massive difference between using AI as a thinking and writing assistant versus generating endless streams of low-effort “AI slop.” I increasingly think people intuitively recognize the distinction immediately. One feels authored, refined, and connected to a real human perspective. The other feels synthetic, generic, emotionally hollow, and mass-produced. The important question is not “Was AI involved?” but rather: “Was a human actually thinking?”

item.title: Games Are Honest. Gamification Often Isn’t.
item.description: This is a short article describing what I think is the difference between a game vs 'gamification' and why that distinction matters. It's parly a rant. Opinions are my own.

item.title: What Strategy Is Actually Betting On
item.description: Strategy’s Bitcoin treasury strategy is not magic and it is not an “infinite money glitch.” It is a directional bet that Bitcoin will appreciate faster than the company’s cost of capital over long periods of time. Products like STRC are built on that assumption. While Bitcoin remains extremely volatile, its long-term performance has dramatically outpaced many traditional assets over the past decade. Strategy is attempting something historically unusual: building a modern corporate capital structure around a volatile monetary asset. Whether that succeeds long term remains an open question, but understanding the mechanics matters far more than hype or slogans.

item.title: Sovereignty Requires Better Failure Modes
item.description: Today’s Continuum work reinforced an important idea: sovereign systems are not defined only by their capabilities, but by how they behave when dependencies are missing, environments change, or systems partially fail.

item.title: PDF Export Consistency Matters More Than It Sounds
item.description: Today Continuum reached an important usability milestone: PDF exports now work consistently across all supported build types instead of depending entirely on one rendering engine. This sounds small on the surface, but it represents something much larger about portability, durability, and local-first software design.

item.title: Continuum v1.6.7.7 — Toward a Fully Encrypted Sovereign Workspace
item.description: Today's work pushed Continuum further toward becoming a true sovereign local-first workspace. The focus was not on “cloud sync” or engagement mechanics. It was on reducing plaintext exposure, improving encrypted workspace handling, and making the local environment more durable and portable across different deployment models. Another important milestone: PDF exports are now supported consistently across all build types, including environments where WeasyPrint is unavailable.

item.title: Weekly Recap: Sunday, May 10 – Saturday, May 16, 2026
item.description: This week focused on turning Continuum into a more complete sovereign workspace rather than just a local Nostr publishing application. Major work included building the first fully functional encrypted vault system, expanding multi-protocol signing identity support (PGP, SSH, Bitcoin, and Nostr), refining browser-side verification tools, and implementing a working passwordless authentication flow using signed Nostr challenges. The week also included continued refinement of offline-first workflows, local signing semantics, and identity management UX.

item.title: Continuum Is Quietly Becoming Something Larger
item.description: The addition of Vault v1 reveals a broader pattern inside Continuum’s architecture: the platform is evolving beyond publishing into a unified local-first authority workspace. This article explores how identities, encrypted storage, signing systems, archives, and proofs all converge around the deeper concept of user-controlled authority rather than cloud-managed convenience.

item.title: Why Local-First Software May Matter More in the AI Era
item.description: As AI dramatically lowers the cost of generating content, code, and digital artifacts, the value of trust, authorship, provenance, and local ownership increases. This article explores why local-first systems like Continuum may become more important — not less — in a world dominated by automation and centralized AI infrastructure.

item.title: Continuum Vault - v1 - Monday, May 11, 2026
item.description: Vault v1 became fully operational inside Continuum in under 8 hours. The new system introduces encrypted local vault storage integrated directly into the Continuum workspace, including lock/unlock workflows, CRUD editing, backup support, and master-password-based encryption. More importantly, the milestone reinforces Continuum’s evolution from a publishing platform into a broader local-first authority workspace managing identities, signing systems, encrypted artifacts, and sensitive data under user control.

item.title: Weekly Recap: Sunday, May 3 – Saturday, May 9, 2026
item.description: Back to full health after stomach issues. Focused on enhancing Continuum’s identity and artifact ecosystem: added Nostr proof attestations, imported/listed PGP & Bitcoin identities, SSH support, and “Verify Signed Artifact Bundle.” Polished UI workflows and had a productive Zoom call with another developer to refine priorities and identity flows. Continued planning workspace encryption and secure credential storage.

item.title: Portable Cryptographic Authorship and Multi-Proof Artifact Bundles
item.description: Continuum now supports complete end-to-end signed artifact bundles containing Nostr, PGP, Bitcoin, and SSH proofs alongside manifests and signer metadata. The verifier flow locally recomputes the artifact hash and validates every attached proof against the same artifact, allowing independently verifiable multi-proof attestations without relying on relays, cloud services, or centralized platforms. The article explores how portable cryptographic artifact bundles, multi-party attestations, and composite signing authorities could fundamentally change digital authorship and verification.

item.title: Beyond Events: Using Nostr Keys as General Signing Identities
item.description: After adding support for PGP and Bitcoin signing identities inside Continuum, a larger realization emerged: Nostr private keys are not limited to signing relay events. They can function as generalized Schnorr signing authorities capable of attesting to external artifacts, authentication challenges, and far more.

item.title: Sovereign Signing Identities — Detached Signatures, Sovereign Identity, and Continuum
item.description: Continuum is increasingly evolving into a local-first workspace for sovereign signing identities. This article explores the philosophical and architectural implications of unifying PGP, Bitcoin, Nostr, and eventually SSH identities under a shared challenge → sign → verify model.

item.title: Portable Artifact Attestations with PGP, Bitcoin, and ... Nostr?
item.description: What started as a simple PGP import feature inside Continuum unexpectedly evolved into a full local-first artifact attestation system supporting PGP, Bitcoin, and Nostr signing identities. This article explores detached signatures, portable proof bundles, and the realization that Nostr private keys can sign far more than relay events.

item.title: Week of April 26–May 2 — Bitcoin 2026
item.description: This week included Bitcoin 2026, real conversations, a few book sales, and a physical reset I didn’t expect. Not everything went smoothly — but I showed up, and that matters.

item.title: Sabbath Is Not About Doing Nothing
item.description: I’ve been rethinking Sabbath — not as stopping work, but as stepping out of the pressure behind it.

item.title: Unfinished Work Is Not Failure
item.description: It’s harder to rest when things are left unfinished. But maybe that’s exactly the point — learning to step away without needing everything to be resolved.

item.title: Week of April 19–25 — Quiet Progress That Actually Matters
item.description: This week wasn’t about big new features. It was about clarity — tightening the message, improving the sites, and getting back into a real writing rhythm. Not flashy, but necessary.

item.title: “Not a Panacea” — And Not Trying to Be
item.description: Reflecting on the statement “Bitcoin is not a panacea” and clarifying what Bitcoin is—and is not—designed to solve.

item.title: Two Models of Money
item.description: An exploration of two fundamentally different approaches to money: deterministic systems governed by fixed rules, and managed systems governed by human decision-making.

item.title: A Brief Conversation on Bitcoin
item.description: A short reflection on a brief, informal conversation with a Federal Reserve professional—highlighting a few key perspectives on Bitcoin, including the institutional framing around Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT).

item.title: Write Once… Render Everywhere?
item.description: Nostr promises “write once, publish everywhere.” And at the protocol level, that’s mostly true. But in practice, how your content *renders* depends heavily on the client. Primal favors Markdown images but ignores HTML. Coracle supports HTML but doesn’t fully render Markdown image syntax. The result: the same post can look completely different depending on where it’s viewed. Continuum takes a different approach—supporting both formats and separating authorship from rendering.

item.title: Who Owns Your Identity?
item.description: California’s digital ID in Apple Wallet is sleek, fast, and convenient—but it reveals a deeper shift: identity is moving from something you *possess* to something a system must *verify*. This article contrasts that model with a self-sovereign approach—where you control your identity and present verifiable proofs when needed. The difference isn’t just technical. It’s foundational.

item.title: Local-First Isn’t the Hard Part
item.description: I built Continuum with ownership and control at the center. Over time, I’ve realized that the real challenge isn’t the local-first model itself—it’s designing something that is just as simple and intuitive as the alternatives without giving up those principles.

item.title: Convenience Matters — But Not at the Cost of Freedom
item.description: Convenience is not the problem. It’s the reason most tools get adopted. But when convenience is built on top of systems that remove control from the user, the tradeoff becomes invisible—until it isn’t. This piece explores that tension and why it’s worth solving, not rejecting.

item.title: The Tradeoff Most People Never See
item.description: Most people aren’t choosing convenience over control consciously. The tradeoff is rarely visible at the moment of decision. This piece explores why that happens and what it means.

item.title: How a Circular Economy Actually Forms - Bitcoin Circular Economy, Part 3
item.description: A circular economy forms when value continues moving between participants instead of exiting the system. The key is not adoption alone, but the persistence of exchange within the network.

item.title: Why Token “Economies” Aren’t Real Economies - Bitcoin Circular Economy, Part 2
item.description: Many systems labeled as “economies” are driven by incentives and token speculation rather than real exchange. A true economy is built on the exchange of goods and services, not the circulation of a token.

item.title: Stop Converting Back - Bitcoin Circular Economy, Part 1
item.description: A circular economy doesn’t require new infrastructure. It starts when individuals stop immediately converting sats back to fiat and allow value to continue moving between participants.

item.title: Why Open Source Still Matters
item.description: Summary: Open source is fundamentally about visibility—the ability to inspect, run, and verify how a system works. In an increasingly opaque software landscape, that transparency matters more than ever. Continuum aligns with these principles, but openness must be introduced at the right time, when the system is coherent and understandable. The goal is not just to expose code, but to make the system truly knowable and trustworthy.

item.title: Direct, Not Intermediated
item.description: Summary: Setting up direct book purchases is not about replacing platforms like Amazon, but about restoring direct relationships between author and reader. Platforms optimize for efficiency but abstract the interaction. Direct purchase introduces a small amount of friction, but creates a more intentional and transparent exchange. It reflects a broader principle behind Continuum: ownership and connection should not be fully intermediated.

item.title: A Place for the Work to Live
item.description: Summary: Adding a Books section to mycontinuum.xyz is a small structural change, but an important one. It creates a place for the ideas behind Continuum—sovereignty, authorship, and ownership—to live alongside the software itself. Code shows what is possible. Writing explains why it matters. Both are necessary, and bringing them together marks a shift from a project to a growing body of work.

item.title: The Sovereign Small-App Pattern
item.description: A 1 1/2 hour experiment building a tiny admin tool for Kiki’s Kitchen Table revealed a repeatable architectural pattern for sovereign business applications: local editing, deterministic data, template rendering, and static deployment.

item.title: Write Anywhere. Publish Anywhere.
item.description: On April 19, 2026, I ran a simple but revealing experiment: create and sign a note offline on one machine, move it physically, and publish it from another. No cloud. No sync service. No account login. What happened wasn’t just a workflow—it was proof of a different model for authorship.

item.title: Finished, Not Complete - Learning to Be Satisfied Without Immediate Results
item.description: Some work is finished before it is complete. This piece explores the difference between completing the work itself and seeing the results of that work—and why learning to be satisfied in that gap is essential.

item.title: The Work That Remains When Striving Ends
item.description: When the pressure to prove something fades, the work does not stop—it becomes clearer and more grounded. This piece reflects on meaningful progress, including recent Continuum releases and foundational improvements, done without striving but not without purpose or responsibility.

item.title: Rest Is Not the Absence of Work (it Is the Absence of Labor)
item.description: Rest is not about stopping activity—it’s about ceasing from striving. This piece explores what it means to continue building, writing, and creating without the burden of pressure, while letting the weight of labor fall away.

item.title: The Rise of Sovereign Micro-Applications
item.description: Modern tools make it possible to build small sovereign applications extremely quickly, enabling a new model for software development.

item.title: Who Continuum Is For
item.description: Continuum is for people and organizations who need durable authorship, self-custodied identity, and a local-first publishing workflow. It is especially well suited for writers, journalists, researchers, human rights activists, ministries, and developers who want to create, preserve, and publish work without depending entirely on centralized platforms.

item.title: Local Files vs Local-First: Why Continuum Exists
item.description: At first glance, Continuum may appear unnecessary to people who already write documents locally on their computers. After all, anyone can open Word, Markdown, or a text editor and save files in a folder. But Continuum is not merely a place to store documents. It is a local-first authorship and publishing system built around identity, verifiable authorship, durable archives, and intentional distribution. This article explains why the difference matters.

item.title: Imagining a Decentralized Alternative to Zipcar, Uber, and Lyft
item.description: Zipcar, Uber, and Lyft promise convenience but leave us vulnerable to breakdowns and opaque rules. By imagining a decentralized alternative — built on peer-to-peer listings, portable reputation, and Bitcoin payments — we can see how transportation could become more resilient, transparent, and sovereign.

item.title: Sovereignty and AI
item.description: An exploration of how AI can serve as an amplifier for builders and writers without displacing human authority. This article argues for a sovereignty-first approach to AI, where ownership, authorship, and identity remain centered in the individual.

item.title: From Blog.StantonWeb to Continuum Stories — Getting the Pipeline Working in a New Repo - Part 2
item.description: In Part 1, I described the architecture behind a decentralized publishing pipeline using Nostr, GitHub Actions, and GitHub Pages. In this follow-up, I walk through what it actually took to get that same pipeline working again in a new repository — including authentication issues, workflow permissions, and the small but critical details that make it all run automatically.

item.title: From Blog.StantonWeb to Continuum Stories - Reusing a Decentralized Publishing Pipeline
item.description: Last summer, I built a decentralized blog using Nostr, GitHub Actions, and GitHub Pages. I’m now taking that same idea and applying it directly to Continuum — turning tagged articles into a live, automatically updating site with zero manual publishing steps.

item.title: The software is beginning to catch up with the book
item.description: A reflection on how the recent build, packaging, and distribution work is helping Continuum better embody the principles laid out in *Building Continuum: Local Authority in a Networked World*.

item.title: My first sats purchase of "Building Continuum"
item.description: Today I received the first sats purchase of *Building Continuum: Local Authority in a Networked World*. More than the amount, it felt like the first real signal that the work is reaching people in a way that aligns with its core message.

item.title: Cross-platform support makes "Building Continuum" tangible
item.description: Today felt like a real threshold moment for Continuum. Cross-platform support across macOS, Windows, Docker, and local-host environments makes the ideas in *Building Continuum: Local Authority in a Networked World* far more tangible.

item.title: Why I Certified the Mac App
item.description: A reflection on why signing and notarizing the native Mac application was a critical milestone for Continuum. This piece explores friction, trust, user onboarding, and why local-first software must feel as smooth and trustworthy as cloud-native products.

item.title: Building vs Writing
item.description: A personal reflection on the past few weeks spent focused on native builds and release infrastructure, and why that did not represent a pause in creativity but a shift from writing into another form of authorship: building the vessel that carries the words.

item.title: “Nostr Clients Are Enough”
item.description: Nostr clients are powerful — especially for discovery and interaction. But they are not designed for ownership, authoring, or long-term control. This article explores why clients are essential, but not sufficient, and what changes when you separate discovery from creation.

item.title: Writing Isn’t Publishing
item.description: Most modern systems collapse writing and publishing into a single action. This creates hidden pressure and limits how ideas develop. This article explores why separating authoring from distribution matters — and how local-first systems restore a healthier way to think, write, and share.

item.title: You Shouldn’t Need the Internet to Think
item.description: Most modern tools assume constant connectivity. If the network is down, the system stops working. This article explores how that assumption quietly reshapes how we create, think, and work — and why local-first systems restore something deeper than just offline access.

item.title: You Don’t Own Your Account
item.description: Most people believe they “own” their accounts, data, and identity online. In reality, they have access — not ownership. This article explores how small, everyday failures (like password resets and lockouts) reveal a deeper architectural problem, and why local-first systems change that foundation.

item.title: You Don’t Rise Into Clarity, You Build Into It
item.description: Clarity is not discovered in abstraction. It is formed through the act of building—over time, through resistance, iteration, and commitment.

item.title: Building Without Applause
item.description: The absence of recognition is not a signal to stop. It is often the condition required to build something that lasts.

item.title: Building Is a Form of Stewardship
item.description: Building is not just creation—it is stewardship. It reflects how we manage time, responsibility, and what has been entrusted to us.

item.title: Sabbath Is Not a Break. It’s Alignment.
item.description: Sabbath is not a recovery mechanism for exhaustion—it is a return to alignment with God as the true source. This article explores how rest reframes work, exposes hidden dependencies, and reshapes the way we build, write, and live.

item.title: You Cannot Build What You Refuse to Leave
item.description: Sabbath reveals whether we are building in trust or in control. This article explores how our attachment to work exposes hidden dependencies, and why true durability requires the ability to step away.

item.title: I Don’t Write to Engage. I Write to Clarify.
item.description: Writing shaped by platforms drifts toward performance. This article explores a different approach—writing as a process of clarification, built through notes, refined over time, and aligned with durability rather than engagement.
